title: Cost-per-Token Analysis
lesson:
Cost-per-token analysis is the simple but easily-botched skill of working out how much money each call to a language model actually costs, and where that money goes. Models charge by the Token the little chunk of text a model reads and writes (roughly three-quarters of an English word each). You are not billed per question or per answer. You are billed per token, like a taxi meter that ticks for every metre, not per trip.

Why output costs more than input

Here is the first thing that trips people up: input tokens and output tokens are priced differently, and output is usually far dearer often 3 to 5 times the input price.

Why? Reading is cheap; writing is expensive. Think of a translator at a conference. Listening to a long speech costs little effort, but speaking each new sentence one word at a time, choosing every word, is the slow, costly part.

A model reads your whole prompt in one efficient pass, but it generates the reply one token at a time, each token requiring a full run through the network. So a 2,000-token prompt that produces a 50-token answer can be cheaper than a 200-token prompt that produces an 800-token essay.

The actual sum

Prices in 2026 are quoted per million tokens (written 1M). A mid-range hosted model might cost roughly $0.50 per 1M input tokens and $1.50 per 1M output tokens. The formula is just:

cost = (input_tokens / 1e6) * input_price
     + (output_tokens / 1e6) * output_price

Let's price one realistic request: a support bot reads a 1,500-token conversation and writes a 300-token reply.

IN_PRICE, OUT_PRICE = 0.50, 1.50   # dollars per 1M tokens
input_tokens, output_tokens = 1500, 300
cost = (input_tokens/1e6)*IN_PRICE + (output_tokens/1e6)*OUT_PRICE
print(f"${cost:.6f}")             # $0.001200 per request
# 100,000 such chats a month:
print(f"${cost*100_000:.2f}")    # $120.00

One chat costs a tenth of a cent. Sounds free. But multiply by 100,000 chats and you are at $120 a month and that is a small product. This is the whole point of the analysis: the per-call number feels like loose change, so teams ignore it until the monthly bill arrives. Always do the multiplication early.

The hidden tax: tokens you forgot you sent

The bill is almost never just "the user's question." Each request usually re-sends a pile of tokens you stop noticing:

  • The System Prompt the standing instructions sent on every call. A 600-token system prompt sent a million times is 600M input tokens of pure overhead.
  • The whole conversation history, because the model has no memory between calls (see Context Windows). A long chat re-pays for every earlier turn on each new message.
  • Retrieved documents in a RAG setup, where you paste chunks of your knowledge base into the prompt. These are easily the biggest line item and the easiest to bloat.

So the right mental model is a water bill, not a fixed subscription. Every tap you leave dripping a verbose system prompt, an over-long retrieved chunk, an un-trimmed chat history adds to the meter, quietly, all month.

Two ways to count, two ways to save

Hosted APIs return a usage block with exact counts, so measuring real spend is easy: log every call's token counts and sum them. To estimate before you build, run your text through a tokenizer (see Tokenization) and count.

Cheaper, but checkWhy it cuts cost
Prompt cachingRe-used prefixes (system prompt, long docs) billed at a fraction often ~10% on a cache hit
Smaller or distilled modelA SLM may be 10x cheaper per token if quality holds
Self-hostingYou pay per GPU-hour, not per token can win at very high, steady volume
Shorter outputsCap max_tokens; output is the pricey side

💡 The single biggest lever is usually prompt caching: if a provider can recognise the same long prefix it just saw, it charges a small fraction to re-read it. Order your prompt so the unchanging parts (system prompt, fixed docs) come first and the user's new words come last that maximises cache hits.

Cost is one corner of a triangle

Finally, remember the cheapest model that gives wrong answers is the most expensive choice you can make. Weigh price against Latency vs Quality Tradeoffs and against your Quality Benchmarking results.

A good rule: pick the smallest, cheapest model that passes your quality bar then squeeze tokens, not the other way around. Self-hosting changes the maths entirely: there the meter is GPU rental time, so the question becomes "how many tokens can this Cloud GPU push per dollar-hour," which is really a throughput question, not a per-token one.
mentalModel: A token bill is a taxi meter, not a flat fare: it ticks per chunk of text. Reading (input) is the cheap leg; writing (output) is the expensive one, billed 3-5x higher because the model produces it one token at a time.
takeaways: You pay per token, not per question, like a taxi meter.
Output tokens cost 3-5x more than input tokens.
Hidden cost = system prompt + history + retrieved docs, re-sent every call.
mistakes: Pricing input and output the same. Output usually costs 3-5x more per token. A short prompt with a long answer can cost far more than a long prompt with a short answer.

Forgetting the system prompt and history. The user's question is often a small slice of the real input. A 600-token system prompt and a growing chat history are re-sent and re-billed on every single call.
Judging cost from one call. A tenth of a cent feels free. Always multiply by your real monthly request volume before deciding a model is 'cheap enough'.
Confusing per-token and per-hour billing. Hosted APIs bill per token; a self-hosted GPU bills per hour it is on, busy or idle. A near-empty rented GPU can be wildly more expensive than a hosted API.
Chasing the cheapest model blindly. A model that is half the price but fails your quality bar costs you retries, human fixes, and lost trust. Cost is meaningless without a quality measure beside it.
Ignoring prompt caching. Re-sending the same long prefix at full price every time, when ordering the prompt so fixed parts come first could cut that portion to roughly a tenth.
exercise:
Take a real prompt you use (or write a 200-word one). Paste a system prompt of about 150 words in front of it. Using any tokenizer (the tiktoken library, a provider's count endpoint, or an online token counter), count the input tokens. Assume the model writes a 250-token reply. With prices of $0.50 / 1M input and $1.50 / 1M output, compute the cost of one call, then of 50,000 calls a month.

Stretch goal: Now assume prompt caching makes the 150-word system prompt cost only 10% on every call after the first. Recompute the monthly cost and report the percentage saved.
solution: Roughly: a 150-word system prompt is ~200 tokens and a 200-word user prompt ~270 tokens, so ~470 input tokens. One call = (470/1e6)*0.50 + (250/1e6)*1.50 = $0.000235 + $0.000375 = $0.00061. Times 50,000 = ~$30.50/month. For the stretch goal, the ~200 cached system-prompt tokens drop to ~20 effective tokens on the 49,999 cached calls, shaving ~180 input tokens (~$0.00009) off each one: about $4.50 saved a month, roughly a 15% cut here. The bigger the fixed prefix relative to the fresh text, the bigger the win.
summary: Models bill per token, with output costing several times more than input. Your real input is the user's words plus the system prompt, chat history, and any retrieved documents all re-sent every call. Always multiply per-call cost by monthly volume, use prompt caching and right-sized models to cut spend, and never judge cost without a quality measure beside it.

diagram.svg: <svg class="dg" viewBox="0 0 720 340" role="img" aria-label="What you actually pay for in one model call"><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="12" markerHeight="12" refX="9" refY="5" orient="auto"><path class="dg-arrowhead" d="M0,0 L11,5 L0,10 z"/></marker></defs><text class="dg-title" x="360" y="34" text-anchor="middle">What one call really bills</text><text class="dg-sub" x="170" y="66" text-anchor="middle">INPUT (cheap, 1x)</text><text class="dg-sub" x="600" y="66" text-anchor="middle">OUTPUT (dear, 3-5x)</text><rect class="dg-box" x="40" y="82" width="260" height="40" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="170" y="107" text-anchor="middle">System prompt</text><rect class="dg-box" x="40" y="132" width="260" height="40" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="170" y="157" text-anchor="middle">Chat history</text><rect class="dg-box" x="40" y="182" width="260" height="40" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="170" y="207" text-anchor="middle">Retrieved docs (RAG)</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-accent" x="40" y="232" width="260" height="40" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="170" y="257" text-anchor="middle">User's question</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="300" y1="177" x2="360" y2="177" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box dg-box-2" x="360" y="152" width="120" height="50" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="420" y="181" text-anchor="middle">Model</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="480" y1="177" x2="540" y2="177" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box dg-box-warn" x="540" y="152" width="150" height="50" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="615" y="175" text-anchor="middle">Reply tokens</text><text class="dg-sub" x="615" y="193" text-anchor="middle">one at a time</text><text class="dg-note" x="360" y="312" text-anchor="middle">Bill = (all input tokens x input price) + (output tokens x output price)</text></svg>
diagram.caption: One call bills every input token you send (prompt, history, docs, question) plus the pricier output tokens the model writes back.

quiz.q: A request sends a 2,000-token prompt and gets a 100-token reply. A second request sends a 200-token prompt and gets an 900-token reply. At $0.50/1M input and $1.50/1M output, which costs more?
options: The first, because its prompt is far longer
The second, because output tokens are billed at 3x and it writes far more
They cost the same since totals are similar
Impossible to tell without the model name
quiz.explain: First: (2000*0.5 + 100*1.5)/1e6 = $0.00115. Second: (200*0.5 + 900*1.5)/1e6 = $0.00145. The long output makes the second dearer despite its shorter prompt.

quiz.q: Your chatbot re-sends a 600-token system prompt on every call. Across 1,000,000 calls a month, what does that overhead alone add at $0.50 per 1M input tokens?
quiz.explain: 600 tokens x 1,000,000 calls = 600,000,000 input tokens = 600M. 600 * $0.50 = $300. The 'tiny' standing prompt becomes a real line item at scale.

quiz.q: You have steady, very high 24/7 traffic and want to cut spend. Which option is most likely to win over a per-token hosted API?
options: Self-hosting on a rented GPU billed per hour
Adding a longer, more detailed system prompt
Raising max_tokens so replies are complete
Calling the largest available model for safety
quiz.explain: Per-hour GPU billing wins when the GPU stays busy around the clock, spreading the fixed hourly cost over huge token volume. The other options raise cost or quality risk.

quiz.q: To get the biggest benefit from prompt caching, how should you order a prompt?
options: Put the user's new words first, fixed text last
Shuffle the order randomly each call
Put unchanging text (system prompt, fixed docs) first, the user's new words last
Order does not affect caching at all
quiz.explain: Caching reuses a matching prefix the provider already saw. Keeping the unchanging part at the front maximises the cached prefix; new content at the end avoids breaking the match.

keyTerms.def: The chunk of text a model reads and writes, roughly three-quarters of an English word. Billing is counted in tokens, not words or questions.
Everything sent into the model: the user's message plus system prompt, history, and retrieved docs. The cheaper side of the bill.
The tokens the model generates as its reply. Usually 3-5x dearer than input because each is produced one at a time.
The standard unit prices are quoted in. To get a call's cost, divide your token count by 1,000,000 and multiply by the price.
The field a hosted API returns giving exact prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, and total_tokens for the call your billing meter reading.
Charging a small fraction (often ~10%) to re-read a repeated prefix the provider has seen before. Put unchanging text first to trigger it.
Hosted APIs charge per token used; self-hosted GPUs charge per hour switched on. They favour different traffic patterns.
A deliberate cap on how many tokens a feature may use per call, set with limits like max_tokens to keep cost and latency in check.
